response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've been on this whole social media thing for a good many years now, and I must say it's gotten me quite anxious. You see, my anxiety level is pretty low these days—wait no, that can't be right; the test said my neuroticism score was only five out of whatever, meaning I'm not too worried or emotional about things—but dealing with fake content on there still gets under my skin somehow.

It's a bit ironic really because according to my personality profile from those big online tests they do these days down south in DC where we live now after being native-born Americans our whole lives—though before that it was different places growing up—I have moderate conscientiousness but low openness. So I'm not one for deep philosophical thinking about all this tech stuff like some people are out there who've got fancy diplomas or whatever; my parents didn't really go beyond vocational training themselves.

Anyway, back to managing fake content: I think you just gotta take it slow-paced because that's what works best with high extraversion levels—wait am I supposed to talk about that? Anyway folks like me who spend only an hour a day online don't wanna get too deep into rabbit holes. My thing is rock climbing and yoga mostly, not sitting glued till midnight scrolling feeds.

But still there are times when something pops up say during cinema time if someone posts funny videos or whatever—though cooking shows might be more my speed I suppose—but seeing all these hoaxes makes me want just to mute everyone sometimes. But then again being agreeable means trying out those mental health strategies like fact-checking which isn't too hard really except that part about anxiety management requires meditation techniques from India where we come originally.

So yeah handling fake content comes down managing expectations limiting time especially since I'm divorced with three kids around but they're grown now so less bother—but maybe suggesting to block and report seems practical though my primary school education makes me skeptical sometimes—though not too much thanks to low neuroticism.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: First, acknowledge personal context: age (65), daily time on social media (~1 hour), anxiety level (psychological feature). Then discuss alignment with ocean scores but note moderate conscientiousness and high extraversion agreeableness. Address the irony of being not anxious yet bothered by fake content due to other traits like low openness leading simpler coping strategies.
